steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Grasby et al.’s research question is whether perceived category similarity predicts consumers’ likelihood of purchasing brand extensions. The team already calculated probability changes for 30 extended-brand pairs. The missing piece is a direct measure of how similar consumers perceive the two product categories to be.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Have households rate category similarity, then correlate those ratings with the probability changes': This supplies the missing variable — a direct consumer rating of category similarity — and connects it to the probability data already in hand. That directly tests the research question. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Poll households on degree of brand recognition, then correlate with average product cost': Brand recognition is not category similarity, and average cost is unrelated to purchase probability. Neither variable addresses the research question. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Have households rate similarity of one product to other products in the same category': This measures within-category similarity, not between-category similarity (e.g., shoes vs. gloves). It does not test whether different categories feel related to consumers. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Poll households on brand recognition, then correlate with purchase frequency of a different group': Brand recognition still isn’t category similarity, and using a different group’s purchase data introduces a mismatch. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
